messages.content:
what type of data can be used in asystematic review  from the following part of a scientific  article “Results
The group of periodontally healthy individuals (H) was composed of 7 women and 13 men, mean age 39.6 ± 16.7 years (range 18 – 79). The group of subjects with periodontitis § was composed of 10 women and 10 men, mean age 44.4 ± 10.2 years (range 25 – 68). Full- mouth periodontal parameters of the study groups are shown in Table 1. Individuals with periodontitis presented a higher mean PD and CAL when compared to periodontally healthy individuals.
GCF levels of hBD-2 were [mean ± s.d.; median (Q3 – Q1)]: Hh = 18.03 ± 28.11 (4.80; 0.00 – 30.69); Ph = 33.43 ± 6.53 (33.29; 28.04 – 38.25); Pg = 29.60 ± 8.68 (27.56; 23.14 – 35.50); and Pp = 35.10 ± 20.04 (26.20; 23.20 – 42.54) (Figure 1). GCF levels of hBD- 3 were [mean ± s.d.; median (Q3 – Q1)]: Hh = 41.12 ± 120.71 (0.00; 0.00 – 0.00); Ph = 1121.74 ± 565.87 (978.44; 760.48 – 1268.12); Pg = 1424.45 ± 1309.38 (938.19; 806.75 – 1266.38); and Pp = 852.76 ± 1121.98 (613.63; 325.50 – 854.68). It is important to note that 17 hBD-3 samples (17/20) and 6 hBD-2 samples (6/20) in the control group were under the detection limit for the ELISA test. These samples represented 14,38% of the total samples. They were imputed a value of 0 (zero) for statistical analyses purposes.
In the hierarchical modelling, the analysis of the null model showed a significant variance in the values of hBD-2 (p < 0.01) and a marginally significant variance in the values of hBD-3 (p = 0.096) by the individual level (Level 2 groupings), indicating a need for running HLM analyses (Table 2).
Results from the multilevel model showed that peri- odontitis was associated with higher levels of hBD-2 (coefficient 14.68; p = 0.023) and hBD-3 (coefficient 1091.86; p < 0.001). There were no effects of site phe- notype on the GCF levels of hBD-2 (p = 0.266 for Pg and p = 0.625 for Pp) (Table 3) and hBD-3 (p = 0.302 for Pg and p = 0.359 for Pp) (Table 4).” ?

The following data types can be used in a systematic review from the above passage of a scientific article: 

- Demographic data (age, gender)
- Full-mouth periodontal parameters (PD, CAL)
- GCF levels of hBD-2 and hBD-3 (including means, standard deviations, medians, and quartiles)
- Statistical analysis results (such as p-values and coefficients)

i also provide you with the Statistical analysis "Data normality was assessed using the Kolmogorov- Smirnov test. Descriptive analyses were presented for periodontal parameters and levels of hBDs. Descrip- tive and bivariate analyses, as well as the creation of 2 databases using site and individual values of hBD-2 and hBD-3, were performed using statistical software (Statistical Package for Social Sciences, IBM SPSS Sta- tistics for Macintosh version 20, IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA). These databases were then used to perform the multilevel analyses (Hierarchical Linear and Non- linear Modeling, HLM 6.06 Statistical Package, Skokie, IL, USA). The results were considered significant if p < 0.05.
For each defensin, data were hierarchically structured in 2 levels: periodontal sites (Level 1: healthy sites/sites with gingival inflammation but no attachment loss/sites with gingival inflammation and attachment loss) nested within individuals (Level 2: individuals with or without periodontitis). The multilevel structure of analyses in- cluded 80 mean site values (Level 1) from 40 individual values (Level 2). Multilevel linear modelling was used to assess the differences in hDB-2 and hBD-3 expression. During the first stage, a “null model” estimated the basic partition of data variability between the 2 levels before the inclusion of site and individual characteristics were taken into account. Level 1 variables were incorporated into the model. In the sequence, Level 2 variables were incorporated, with the calculation of p-values (Student’s t test). Only variables with p < 0.05 were retained in the final model. The intraclass correlation coefficient, i.e., the proportion of total variance attributed to the cluster level (individual characteristics), was also calculated."  can it be more accurate which type of data to use ?

Based on the additional information provided, the following types of data can be used in a systematic review:

- Data normality assessment results (Kolmogorov-Smirnov test)
- Descriptive analyses of periodontal parameters and levels of hBDs
- Bivariate analyses
- Databases containing site and individual values of hBD-2 and hBD-3
- Multilevel linear modeling results (including coefficients and p-values)
- Intraclass correlation coefficient values

these are the results from another study "Results
Demographics and periodontal parameters
A significant age difference was found between periodontally healthy subjects (25.79 ± 3.95)
and periodontitis patients (NS [40.32 ± 12]; S [43.88 ± 10.64]) (p<0.05). The distribution of the male and female participants had no significant difference between the study groups, periodontally healthy subjects (5 males/15 females) and periodontitis patients (NS - 9 males/16 females; S - 12 males/13 females) (p = 0.28).
The periodontal clinical parameters are presented in table 1. Regarding the full mouth periodontal clinical parameters, PI, BOP, PD and CAL were significantly lower in periodontally healthy subjects than in periodontitis patients (p<0.0001). Only for GI, a significant difference was observed between periodontally healthy patients and smoker patients (p=0.0001). The sampled sites of periodontally healthy subjects presented significantly lower values of PD and CFV than healthy sampled sites of smokers and non-smokers with periodontitis (p<0.05), as well as lower CAL than healthy sampled sites of smokers (p<0.05) (Table 2). In patients with periodontitis, the intra-group comparison revealed lower levels of PD, CAL and CFV in the healthy sampled sites than in the diseased sites (p<0.0001) (Table 2). hBD 1 and hBD 2 quantification. The GCF levels of hBD 1 and hBD 2 are presented in Figure 1. The hBD 1 levels were significantly higher in healthy sites of the patients without periodontal disease than in healthy sites of smokers and non-smokers with periodontitis (p<0.0001), with a mean difference of 31.62 (95% CI: 17.15 - 46.08) between SH and H and a mean difference of 31.20 between NSH and H (95% CI: 13.81 - 48.57). The lowest GCF hBD 2 level was observed in healthy sites of non-smokers (p=0.0003). A mean difference of 33.61 was observed between SH and H (95% CI: -3.41   70.63), and a mean difference of 6.56 was found between NSH and H (95% CI: -1.68   14.79). The magnitude of the effect observed for GCF levels of hBDs in healthy sites was large (eta squared= 0.497 and r= 0.70 for hBD 1; eta squared= 0.176 and r= 0.42 for hBD 2) (17).
The comparison between diseased sites of patients with periodontitis revealed significantly higher levels of hBD 2 and lower levels of hBD 1 in the GCF of smokers compared to non-smokers (p<0.05). The mean difference between SD and NSD was 74.67 (95% CI: -50.82   200.16) for hBD 2 and 6.89 (95% CI: 0.64   13.14) for hBD 1. The magnitude of the effect observed for GCF levels of hBDs. in diseased sites of smokers and non-smokers was intermediate (hBD 2 eta squared= 0.073 and r= 0.27; hBD 1 eta squared= 0.081 and r= 0.28) (17). 
In non-smokers, higher levels of hBD 1 and 2 were observed in diseased sites than in healthy sites (p<0.05). A mean difference of 4.34 for hBD 1 (95% CI: -6.74   15.41) and 118.95 for hBD 2 (95% CI: -3.14   241.04) was observed between NSD and NSH. The magnitude of the effect observed was intermediate for hBD 2 (eta squared= 0.097 and r= 0.31) and small for hBD 1 (eta squared= 0.021 and r= 0.14). However, in smokers, no significant difference in hBDs level was found between healthy and diseased sites (p>0.05). A mean difference of 2.13 for hBD 1 (95% CI: -2.71   6.96) and 10.67 for hBD 2 (95% CI: -36.24   57.58) was observed between SD and SH. The magnitude of the effect observed was small for hBD 2 (eta squared= 0.019 and r= 0.14) and intermediate for hBD 1 (eta squared= 0.108 and r= 0.33) (17)." from this and the previous study what data exactly can be used in a systematic review ?

Based on the two studies, the following types of data can be used in a systematic review:

- Demographic data (such as age and gender)
- Full-mouth periodontal clinical parameters (including PI, BOP, PD, CAL, and GI)
- Gingival crevicular fluid (GCF) levels of hBD-1 and hBD-2
- Intra-group and inter-group comparison of periodontal parameters and GCF levels of hBDs
- Means and standard deviations of periodontal parameters and GCF levels of hBDs for groups with or without periodontitis and with or without smoking status
- Comparisons of GCF levels of hBDs between healthy sites and diseased sites
- Statistical analysis results (such as p-values, mean differences, confidence intervals, eta squared, and r values)
can you provide me with specific results that could be used in a systematic review ?

Sure, here are some specific results from the two studies that could be used in a systematic review:

- Study 1: 

1. Mean age of periodontally healthy individuals (39.6 ± 16.7 years) and individuals with periodontitis (44.4 ± 10.2 years).
2. Full-mouth periodontal parameters of periodontally healthy individuals and individuals with periodontitis, including PD and CAL.
3. GCF levels of hBD-2 and hBD-3 in periodontally healthy individuals and individuals with periodontitis.
4. Hierarchical linear modelling results for hBD-2 and hBD-3, including coefficients and p-values.

- Study 2:

1. Mean age of periodontally healthy individuals (25.79 ± 3.95 years) and individuals with periodontitis (NS [40.32 ± 12]; S [43.88 ± 10.64] years).
2. Full-mouth periodontal clinical parameters including PI, BOP, PD and CAL.
3. GCF levels of hBD-1 and hBD-2 in periodontally healthy individuals and individuals with periodontitis.
4. Intra-group and inter-group comparison of periodontal parameters and GCF levels of hBDs.
5. Means and standard deviations of periodontal parameters and GCF levels of hBDs for groups with or without periodontitis and with or without smoking status.
6. Comparisons of GCF levels of hBDs between healthy sites and diseased sites.
7. Statistical analysis results, including p-values, mean differences and confidence intervals for hBD-1 and hBD-2 in healthy and diseased sites of smokers and non-smokers with periodontitis.
can you produce specific results with numbers from the  GCF levels of hBD-2 from the two studies  ?

Sure, here are specific results with numbers for the GCF levels of hBD-2:

- Study 1: 
GCF levels of hBD-2 [mean ± s.d.; median (Q3 – Q1)]: 
- Hh = 18.03 ± 28.11 (4.80; 0.00 – 30.69)
- Ph = 33.43 ± 6.53 (33.29; 28.04 – 38.25)
- Pg = 29.60 ± 8.68 (27.56; 23.14 – 35.50)
- Pp = 35.10 ± 20.04 (26.20; 23.20 – 42.54)

- Study 2:

1. The lowest GCF hBD 2 level was observed in healthy sites of non-smokers (mean difference of 33.61 with 95% CI of -3.41   70.63, p = 0.0003).
2. In diseased sites of patients with periodontitis, smokers had significantly higher levels of hBD-2 than non-smokers (mean difference of 74.67 with 95% CI of -50.82 - 200.16, p < 0.05).
3. In non-smokers, higher levels of hBD-2 were observed in diseased sites than in healthy sites (mean difference of 118.95 with 95% CI of -3.14 - 241.04, p < 0.05).

Note: The units for GCF levels of hBD-2 were not provided in either study.
please provide results only from healthy patients and no smokers periodontal patients and execute a q-test .

Unfortunately, the information provided in the two studies is not sufficient to provide results for healthy patients and non-smoking periodontal patients only. The studies included multiple groups, including healthy patients, non-smoking periodontal patients, and smoking periodontal patients. However, they did not report results specifically for healthy patients and non-smoking periodontal patients. Therefore, it is not possible to conduct a Q-test with the available information.
please provide results only from healthy patients and diseased sites of non-smoking periodontal patients .

Here are the results for healthy sites and diseased sites of non-smoking periodontal patients:

- Study 2:

1. Healthy sites in non-smoking periodontitis patients had significantly lower levels of hBD-2 compared to healthy sites of patients without periodontal disease (mean difference of 33.61 with 95% CI of -3.41   70.63, p = 0.0003).
2. In non-smoking periodontitis patients, higher levels of hBD-2 were observed in diseased sites compared to healthy sites (mean difference of 118.95 with 95% CI of -3.14 - 241.04, p < 0.05).

Note: The units for GCF levels of hBD-2 were not provided in this study.
please include the values Hh and Pp from the 1st study and values from from healthy patients and diseased sites of non-smoking periodontal patients  from the 2nd study .

Here are the values for GCF levels of hBD-2 from healthy patients and diseased sites of non-smoking periodontal patients:

- For the first study:

  - Hh: 18.03 ± 28.11 (mean ± s.d.)
  - Pp: 35.10 ± 20.04 (mean ± s.d.)
  
- For the second study:

  - Healthy sites in non-smoking periodontitis patients had lower levels of hBD-2 than healthy sites of patients without periodontal disease (mean difference of 33.61 with 95% CI of -3.41   70.63, p = 0.0003).
  - Non-smoking periodontitis patients had higher levels of hBD-2 in diseased sites compared to healthy sites (mean difference of 118.95 with 95% CI of -3.14 - 241.04, p < 0.05).

Note: The units for GCF levels of hBD-2 were not provided in either study.
can the the last results from the two studies be used in a single execution so outcomes can be find out ?

It is not recommended to compare the results for GCF levels of hBD-2 from the two studies directly since they differ in the study design, patient population, and methods used for measuring GCF levels. Additionally, the units for the GCF levels of hBD-2 were not provided in either study, making it difficult to compare the results. 

Systematic reviews aim to evaluate and synthesize the results of multiple studies to provide an overall conclusion. Therefore, a systematic review could include both studies in separate analyses, evaluating the results and the quality of evidence individually for each study to draw conclusions.
